question: Why was the neighbor surprised?
answer_a: He'd never seen Ernie watering the lawn before
answer_b: He accidentally saw Ernie using his gift
responses:
<thinking>
The story’s relevant scene is when Ernie is secretly using the “aqueous fuel catalyst” to fill his car’s gas tank with water. The narration says Ernie had run a garden hose to the car and was worried about being observed: <quote>He looked uneasily up and down the dawning street and was relieved to find it still empty.</quote> That already suggests he knew what he was doing would look strange. Then the neighbor appears: <quote>His neighbor's gate was open and Mr. Jones stood open-mouthed a few feet behind him</quote>. Ernie immediately reacts by hiding what he is actually doing: <quote>Ernie swung the hose around, flipping his thumb over the end to make a spray, and nonchalantly began to water the little rectangle of lawn between sidewalk and curb.</quote> This implies the neighbor had caught him in the act of doing something unusual with the hose and the car.

The neighbor’s response is stronger than mild surprise at lawn care habits: <quote>Mr. Jones voiced no complaint. He backed off several steps, stared intently at Ernie, rather palely</quote> and then <quote>made off... at a very fast shuffle, shaking his feet a little now and then and glancing back several times over his shoulder</quote>. That seems excessive if the only issue were “I’ve never seen Ernie watering the lawn before.” Also, Ernie invents an excuse afterward: <quote>I've just found out that we're supposed to water our lawns only before seven in the morning or after seven in the evenings. It's the law.</quote> The need for a cover story further shows he was concealing something more significant.

Answer A is weak because the text never says Mr. Jones was surprised specifically because Ernie never watered the lawn before. Answer B is better supported because Mr. Jones essentially caught Ernie using the catalyst gift—putting water into the gas tank—and Ernie had to disguise it as lawn watering.
</thinking>
<answer>B</answer>

<thinking>

The narration says Ernie had put the catalyst in the gas tank and then inserted the hose: <quote>He looked uneasily up and down the dawning street</quote> and then <quote>His neighbor's gate was open and Mr. Jones stood open-mouthed a few feet behind him</quote>. That reaction needs explaining. Mr. Jones is not just seeing ordinary lawn watering. He catches Ernie in the middle of something strange: a garden hose is being used at the open gas tank of a car. Ernie immediately tries to cover this up: <quote>Ernie swung the hose around, flipping his thumb over the end to make a spray, and nonchalantly began to water the little rectangle of lawn between sidewalk and curb.</quote> If the real issue were simply that Mr. Jones had never seen Ernie water the lawn before, there would be no need for this sudden disguise.

Mr. Jones’s response also shows he has seen something bizarre, not merely unusual gardening behavior. After getting splashed, <quote>He backed off several steps, stared intently at Ernie, rather palely</quote> and then <quote>made off ... at a very fast shuffle, shaking his feet a little now and then and glancing back several times over his shoulder</quote>. That is much stronger than mild surprise at seeing a neighbor do yard work. Later details reinforce that Mr. Jones remains disturbed: <quote>he heard a window in Mr. Jones's house slam loudly. It unsettled him. Coming home the next day, he caught his sister and Mr. Jones consulting about something</quote>. So the neighbor likely suspects Ernie is doing something crazy or impossible with the car.

Answer A is weak because the text never says Mr. Jones was surprised specifically because Ernie never watered the lawn. Ernie’s own excuse to his sister—<quote>we're supposed to water our lawns only before seven in the morning</quote>—is a cover story, not the narrator’s explanation. The surprise comes from the accidental glimpse of Ernie using the catalyst-powered gift with the car.
</thinking>
<answer>B</answer>

Just before the neighbor appears, Ernie has already put the powder in the tank and inserted the garden hose: <quote>Quickly he dipped up a little of the white powder ... dumped it in the gas tank ... and picked up the spurting hose and jabbed it into the round hole.</quote> Then, <quote>His neighbor's gate was open and Mr. Jones stood open-mouthed a few feet behind him</quote>. That reaction—“open-mouthed”—suggests surprise at what he is seeing right then.

What is Mr. Jones seeing? Not merely lawn-watering, because Ernie only begins pretending to water the lawn after noticing him: <quote>Ernie swung the hose around ... and nonchalantly began to water the little rectangle of lawn between sidewalk and curb.</quote> So the surprising sight came before the cover-up. Mr. Jones likely saw Ernie putting water into the car’s gas tank, which is effectively Ernie using the strange fuel gift.

Answer A is weaker. While it might be true in a vague everyday sense that the neighbor wasn’t used to seeing Ernie water the lawn, the text gives a much more specific reason for his reaction. After being sprayed, Mr. Jones <quote>backed off several steps, stared intently at Ernie, rather palely</quote> and then hurried away, looking back. That is a stronger reaction than simple surprise at lawn care habits. It fits seeing something bizarre or alarming—Ernie apparently fueling a car with a hose from the house.

Therefore the neighbor was surprised because he accidentally witnessed Ernie using the gift.
</thinking>
<answer>B</answer>
